**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci, 2000  
The paper argues that well-being and high-quality motivation depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness rather than external rewards alone. “Interesting research” is valuable insofar as it supports autonomy and competence, while leading a team could also be valuable if it increases autonomy and relatedness.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999  
This meta-analysis finds that tangible, controlling rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. It does not imply salary is irrelevant, but it supports caution about trading intrinsically motivating work for modest extrinsic gains.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007  
This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, social support, and work context predict satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It strongly supports evaluating day-to-day work design rather than just pay, title, or company prestige.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010  
Across many studies, actual pay level has only a modest association with job satisfaction. This suggests a somewhat higher salary should usually be weighed against work content, autonomy, team quality, and role fit.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023  
The paper finds emotional well-being generally keeps rising with income for many people, but with diminishing returns and plateaus among the least happy. It argues against ignoring compensation entirely, but also against treating a salary increase as decisive at already-high income levels.

**From Wealth to Well-Being? Money Matters, but Less Than People Think** — Aknin, Norton & Dunn, 2009  
The paper finds that people tend to overestimate how much income differences translate into happiness differences. It supports the idea that salient compensation gains can be overweighted relative to everyday experience.

**Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction** — Schkade & Kahneman, 1998  
People predicting satisfaction overfocus on salient differences and underweight routine daily experience. In job decisions, this implies salary, title, and company brand may loom larger than the actual tasks, meetings, colleagues, and pace that dominate lived experience.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005  
Person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit are strongly related to satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. “Interestingness” is one kind of fit, but values, team, manager, and role fit also matter.

**Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do** — Amabile, 1997  
The paper argues that creativity is highest when intrinsic motivation, expertise, and creative-thinking skills are supported by the work environment. For research-oriented roles, losing intrinsically interesting problems can be especially costly for creative output.

**Differential Challenge Stressor–Hindrance Stressor Relationships with Job Attitudes, Turnover Intentions, Turnover, and Withdrawal Behavior: A Meta-Analysis** — Podsakoff, LePine & LePine, 2007  
This meta-analysis distinguishes challenge stressors, which can relate positively to motivation and performance, from hindrance stressors, which harm satisfaction and increase turnover intentions. Leading a team may be positive if it feels like responsibility, growth, and influence, but negative if it mainly adds politics, ambiguity, and interruptions.

**Managerial Work: Analysis from Observation** — Mintzberg, 1971  
This observational study describes managerial work as fragmented, interruption-heavy, communication-intensive, and oriented around short activities. It is relevant if the appeal of “leading a small team” comes with less deep technical or research time than expected.

**Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion** — Vallerand et al., 2003  
The paper distinguishes harmonious passion, which supports well-being, from obsessive passion, which can create conflict and burnout. It supports prioritizing genuinely interesting work, but not “almost exclusively” if that sacrifices autonomy, relationships, health, or broader life goals.
